chapter.name: Pushing the king away from the center

chapters.name: The "W-maneuver" aka THE MOST IMPORTANT CHAPTER
Pushing the king away from the center
Now with the dark squared bishop!
Don't twist your head on this one!
What if he/she doesn't play the best moves?

comments.text: Now that you know what positions you have to achieve, it's time to reach it from the starting position. I'll not show the most precise moves (even if I was a computer and knew them all by heart), but rather the the most natural ones. It's here where you have to avoid spending too much tempi, because the 50-move-draw rule is out there. This is all about piece coordination and controlling territory.

First step: Put your king on the center! There are no concrete moves to follow as we saw in the "W-maneuver", only ideas, and you can do this at your own. I'll show some of them to help you. Remember the keys squares!
Black is preventing the White's king to come closer, so you have to use your pieces to make progress. Take away the central squares from Black!

Now the second piece comes in the play. The centralization of your pieces generally is enough to push away the enemy king. You have to keep control of squares without giving Black a "outflanking" opportunity.
Remember this pattern. It's often used the in "King + Bishop pair vs King" checkmate procedure. You're kicking Black king further back. Sometimes, you can even use a bishop defended by a knight.

Some players had difficulties in situations like this in human play. What should we do if the enemy king goes to the "right corner" by itself? How to trap it in this situation? The answer: It's already trapped, we're going to do a "W-maneuver" via another route. It's worth spending some time (if you have any) to accurately determine the correct sequence of moves.

The most difficult part here is that we have to set up "W-maneuver" from the middle stage. You have to be confident in the power of your pieces: Even you're not intending it, they already take control of many squares. The enemy king can't run away! Just adapt your plans!
These are human moves.
Whenever you have the chance to take more squares away from the enemy, do it! In this case, White already has control over one of the key squares.
The key square for White's king!
Sometimes, you might want to force the enemy king back to the "wrong corner" in order to set up the w-maneuver properly.
And here we go, with Ne5 and Nf7 coming next, it's easy from here.

Now we got our dream position, and from here the checkmate should be pretty much automatic if you got the hang of it. Let me emphasize that this isn't the most precise move order, the idea is to reach our "dream position" by using a coherent and easy plan, there is no use to be fancy when your clock is ticking here.

Generally speaking, in less than 20 moves you're able to put the enemy king on the edge of the board. If you don't fool around from the starting position, you'll always be able to checkmate in less than 40 moves.
The knight reachs its key square! And of course, it cuts off the h8 square.
Looks familiar?
Not the most accurate defense by Black, this will help you to accelerate your plan.
Now we'll again set up the cage.
The cage is created! Knight on d7, bishop on d3, both on light squares. If you read chapter 10 carefully, you'll already know why.
We have to waste a tempo to give Black the move and tighten the cage.
Taking c6 away! It's becoming easier...
Remember this important move. Whenever Black "threatens" to escape, shut the door!
The same pattern again.
As we saw before, this line doesn't help Black either.
Whenever the Black king tries to move away from the "right corner", continuing with the "W-maneuver" is enough to put it back right to the track.
Now it's time to mate! You can spend some seconds here to calculate the mate, but with practice, you'll know how to apply it without needing to think.
Don't forget: Just play this knight move when it comes WITH CHECK!
I mentioned this line earlier in this study. It's one move longer.
Stalemate. Make sure the knight is coming with check.
This is just a matter of taste. Like we've seen earlier, GM Alexandra Kosteniuk preferred to checkmate from the distance.
Quite pretty too.
Avoiding stalemate.
Now it comes with CHECK! Make sure that happens.

34 moves, with small inaccuracies from both sides. From the worst winning position, the superior side mates within ~33 moves with optimal play. But we're not engines, and it's not necessary to be one. No one with the bare king would be such a great defender to find ways to extend the defeat for two or three moves. Believe in yourself!

lines.t: first!
Guys, don't forget to tell me if you any of find mistakes, specially in the "interactive lesson".

lines.t: I struggled with the official tutorial based on deletang, because I felt they skipped a lot of "what if he does this"
Managed to learn this method, thanks a bunch!
One thing I'm still not confident about is phase 1, where you push king into wrong corner. What do if he retreats to edge or right corner
and tries to outflank you?

lines.t: Really good study! I also love KNB vs K.
I usually move Nd7 > f6 > d5 and create cage in chapter 10 ( 7.Nf6 Kc7 8.Nd5+ Kd8 9.Kd6 Kc8 10.Ke7 not best but my pattern ).
I wrote articles about KNB vs K( from part1 to 7 ).  https://chesski.blogspot.com/2012/08/k-n-b-vs-k.html
It's written in japanese, however I'm happy if diagrams give  you ideas ( especially part 5, 6 and 7 ).
Chapter 9 - Key position: b1-h7 diagonal rather than a2-h7
Correct chapter 9 has a typo.
Thank you very much, nice method!!! learned in less than 2 hours... Just need to practice some more to be a little faster!!
Thanks guys! I corrected them now!

lines.t: turkyem021 Call the entire study as "bad" because there are even more quicker ways to checkmate in some positions is somewhat strange,
lines.title: CM

lines.t: Because I made several warnings about other ways along the study. I wanted to be coherent on it, I used the easier plan, as the title says.
lines.title: CM

lines.t: I allowed the engine for the users to see alternative lines if they want to do so. I'm pretty sure that you used it too.
lines.title: CM

lines.t: Anyway, I appreciate some of your suggestions, and I'll add one of them in the study as I said before.
